policy_statement: Hong Kong targets ceasing new registration of fuel-propelled and hybrid private cars in 2035 or earlier.
mechanism_description: It pursues ceasing new registration of fuel-propelled and hybrid private cars, accelerating a shift to electric cars and lowering transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Vehicle market regulation (sales/registration phaseout) → fleet electrification → ↓ fossil fuel combustion in road transport → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Regulations end (or set an end-date for) the sale/first registration of new ICE and hybrid private cars, reshaping vehicle purchase choices and manufacturer offerings toward battery-electric vehicles.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of gasoline/diesel in the light-duty fleet lowers CO₂ (and co-emitted pollutants); upstream emissions shift to the electricity system and are typically lower and fall further as the grid decarbonizes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions from the transport sector, contributing to lower cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is cutting road-transport GHG emissions by eliminating new ICE/hybrid car sales and shifting the vehicle fleet toward zero-emission (battery-electric) vehicles.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Market/sales restriction → accelerated EV uptake → reduced fossil-fuel combustion → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can also improve air quality or energy efficiency, the mechanism’s principal intent and largest climate effect is direct emissions mitigation from transport fuel switching.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity grid is very carbon-intensive, near-term net reductions can be smaller, but the policy still targets emissions mitigation via electrification; hybrid inclusion/exclusion changes magnitude, not the primary pathway.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that phases out new ICE/hybrid private car sales/registration operates through the same dominant pathway—constraining fossil-fueled vehicle additions to the fleet to reduce transport emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Details like whether it is economy-wide mitigation vs. transport-specific, and supporting measures (charging, incentives) are policy-level specifics handled separately.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge cases include jurisdictions with high-carbon electricity (reduced near-term net benefit), loopholes (e.g., exemptions, plug-in hybrids treated as ZEV), or shifts to older used ICE imports that slow fleet turnover; these affect effectiveness but not the primary mitigation classification.
key_indicators: “Roadmap on Popularisation of Electric Vehicles”; “ceasing the new registration of fuel-propelled and hybrid private cars in 2035 or earlier”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollution (NOx/PM) and noise from road transport; potential energy security benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: If “ceasing new registration” is enacted as a legally binding prohibition, it could be coded as a regulatory-mandate; the excerpt frames it as a roadmap/announced timeline, so target-setting is used here.
